input.initialPrompt:
I'm setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11 from scratch. I need
to target both Android and Windows. I can't run commands right now — give
me a complete step-by-step plan with all the PowerShell commands I'll need,
including how to discover the right versions dynamically.

data.content:
Here's a complete setup plan for .NET MAUI on Windows 11 targeting Android + Windows.

---

## Step 1 — Install the .NET SDK

```powershell
# Download and run the official installer
winget install Microsoft.DotNet.SDK.9
# Verify
dotnet --version
```

## Step 2 — Install the MAUI workload

```powershell
# List available workloads to confirm the name
dotnet workload search maui

# Install it (includes Android, Windows, iOS stubs)
dotnet workload install maui

# Verify installed workloads
dotnet workload list
```

## Step 3 — Install Android SDK dependencies

The MAUI workload installs Android SDK components automatically, but you may need to accept licenses and verify paths.

```powershell
# Find where the Android SDK was provisioned
$androidSdk = Join-Path $env:LOCALAPPDATA "Microsoft\Android\sdk"
if (!(Test-Path $androidSdk)) {
    # Fallback: check the dotnet-managed path
    $androidSdk = & dotnet --info |
        Select-String "Android" |
        ForEach-Object { $_.ToString().Trim() }
    Write-Host "Android SDK location: $androidSdk"
}

# Accept all Android SDK licenses
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --licenses

# List installed Android SDK packages to verify
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list_installed
```

## Step 4 — Install Java JDK (if not bundled)

.NET 9+ bundles the Microsoft OpenJDK. Verify it:

```powershell
# Check if MAUI installed its own JDK
$jdkPath = Join-Path $env:LOCALAPPDATA "Microsoft\Android\jdk"
if (Test-Path $jdkPath) {
    Write-Host "Bundled JDK found at $jdkPath"
} else {
    # Install manually
    winget install Microsoft.OpenJDK.17
}
```

## Step 5 — Enable Windows App SDK (for Windows target)

```powershell
# The MAUI workload includes Windows support, but verify:
dotnet workload list | Select-String "maui-windows"

# If missing:
dotnet workload install maui-windows
```

## Step 6 — Enable Developer Mode (required for Windows deployment)

```powershell
# Enable Developer Mode via registry
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock" `
    /t REG_DWORD /f /v "AllowDevelopment" /d 1
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock" `
    /t REG_DWORD /f /v "AllowAllTrustedApps" /d 1

# Or open Settings UI directly
start ms-settings:developers
```

## Step 7 — Create and build a test project

```powershell
# Discover available MAUI templates
dotnet new list maui

# Create a new project
dotnet new maui -n HelloMaui
cd HelloMaui

# Build for Windows
dotnet build -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Build for Android
dotnet build -f net9.0-android
```

## Step 8 — Set up the Android Emulator

```powershell
# List available system images dynamically
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list |
    Select-String "system-images;android-"

# Install a system image (pick the latest stable API level from output above)
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" `
    "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64"

# Create an AVD
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat" create avd `
    -n "Pixel_MAUI" -k "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64" `
    --device "pixel_6"

# Launch it
& "$androidSdk\emulator\emulator.exe" -avd Pixel_MAUI
```

## Step 9 — Run the app

```powershell
# Run on Windows
dotnet run -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Run on Android emulator (ensure emulator is running)
dotnet run -f net9.0-android
```

## Step 10 — Verify everything

```powershell
# One-liner health check
dotnet workload list; dotnet --info
```

---

**Key notes:**
- Replace `net9.0` with `net10.0` etc. if you installed a different SDK — `dotnet --version` tells you.
- The `--list` flags on `sdkmanager` and `dotnet workload search` let you discover correct versions dynamically instead of hardcoding them.
- If you prefer Visual Studio instead, `winget install Microsoft.VisualStudio.2022.Community` with the ".NET MAUI" workload selected covers all of the above automatically.
